author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
What It is to Accomplish What is Good.
title: What It is to Accomplish What is Good.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And now does not what follows most plainly show whence he spoke?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For I know that in me, that is, in my flesh, dwelleth no good thing”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For if he had not explained what he said by the addition of “that is, in my flesh,” it might, perchance, be otherwise understood, when he said, “in me.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And therefore he repeats and urges the same thing in another form: “For to will is present with me, but to perform that which is good is not.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For this is to perform that which is good, that a man should not even lust.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For the good is incomplete when one lusts, even although a man does not consent to the evil of lust.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For the good that I would,” says he, “I do not; but the evil that I would not, that I do.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Now, if I do that I would not, it is no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This he repeated impressively, and as it were to stir up the most slothful from slumber: “I find then that the law,” said he, “is for me wishing to do good, since evil is present with me.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: The law, then, is for one who would do good, but evil is present from lust, though he does not consent to this who says, “It is no longer I that do it.”
